sentences.text: In the 19th of January, 1940.
So right after the war started.
Yes.
The war started.
And I came from school.
And we got married.
And then the Germans again, yeah.
In the meantime, we escaped from this city, because when the communists came, they decided that my husband's father is an owner of a big store.
So they wanted to put everybody in jail, you know.
So we escaped to a little town to the east.
And then we came back when the Germans came to our city, because we didn't want to stay.
And my husband was working at this time in the hospital.
No.
No ghettos, yet.
And then later, little by little, they started to squeeze everybody.
They put the people into ghettos, you know.
Yeah, the story started.
In 1940, we were under Russian occupation.
And then the war started between the Russians and Germans.
So the Germans pushed the Russians away to the other front.
And so we went back, me and my husband, to Przemysl, our city.
And we started to, you know, they started to put us to ghetto.
They started first to take out most of the people.
And they called it to work.
But this was not work.
It was 5,000 people-- the first-- how do you say it in English?
They took them to Auschwitz.
Transport?
Transport to Auschwitz.
And my husband, at this time a young guy, was taken to work camp.
And they were cutting the trees in this camp.
I find it out where they took him.
And I started to send money there.
First of all, they took all the kids-- the mothers with the children.
And they took them to like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another place next to Przemysl.
And they killed them.
So we knew they were not taking anybody to work.
So at this time, they formed the Judenrat, Jewish people that were supposed to govern the Jews in the ghetto.
This was our president.
And they were working with Germans.
Germans were coming-- we need 10,000 Jews.
We need 5,000.
And this is how they were going to the concentration camps.
In the meantime, the few people that they needed to work in Przemysl like for military purposes, they had a military base there.
So I was working there.
And when I found out where my husband is, I went to visit him.
First, I was sending him the money.
Then I tried to arrange to get him out of this working camp.
So where am I going to get him out?
To a ghetto?
In the meantime, I went there.
And they caught me there in this camp.
This was a working camp.
What was the name of that camp?
That came-- wait a minute.
Let me remember.
This was Huta Komorowska.
It was a few camps that they were using Jewish labor.
And the Jews lived in the camp there.
And they were working.
And the Germans were shooting.
And they were working-- just going like this.
And when I came there, I had already false papers in the name of Helena Mayorska.
I obtained those papers through the Polish underground.
And when we were still living in Przemysl before they took us to the ghetto, we had a big house.
And the Germans took a few rooms for the people that they wanted to put up.
And we got the guy that was working in the Polish underground.
We didn't know it.
He was the German police.
But he really was working for the Polish underground.
His name was Zaremba.
This I remembered.
And I will always remember.
And he and his friend were living in the next apartment.
And once my brother came home-- this was before the ghetto, you see.
And my brother came home and said, I don't know what's happening.
But there's so many Gestapo police at our neighbors house-- where the other guy lives.
So the first thing that I was thinking, I'm going to Zaremba's quarters to see what he has there.
They're looking for something.
I came there.
And on the desk, there were some papers.
And they apparently were papers from this Polish underground-- the connection.
So I, not thinking, took this paper and put it inside the wood stock like in the kitchen everybody had.
And in a few minutes, the Gestapo was in our house.
And they were looking in Zaremba's quarters.
They didn't find anything.
So when he came home, they are arrested the other guy.
But they didn't arrest him, because they didn't find anything.
And he said that I saved his life.
And he procured for me the kennkarte, the false papers.
And he said that I should leave Przemysl because at this time, I was already in the ghetto later.
He said, it is very dangerous.
They're going to finish this ghetto.
You have to get out of the ghetto.
So he gave me the papers.
And in the early morning, he took me to the train.
He was escorting a transport of people from the police to Krakau to the-- how you call this in English?
I don't know.
And I was going with the train with him to this.
From there, I went to Zakopane.
And he was escorting me in his attire-- a German policeman.
And I wanted to go through to Czechoslovakia and Hungary to escape from Poland.
But before that, I wanted to go to see my husband and to leave with him some money that he can survive.
So I went there.
And they caught me.
And this was the story.
I landed in the camps.
Well, continue.
After you were caught, what happened?
After I was caught, I was in this camp and working in the office all of a sudden, because I knew German.
I was fluent in German.
I had exams in Polish high school in German too.
And after that, what did they do after that?
Oh, then they came one day.
And they took away my husband.
My husband was in this camp.
They took my husband away to Rzeszow because they had an outbreak of typhus.
And my husband was very famous to cure the people with this.
And this was [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know the German word describing it as such.
So then we started a long journey through all the camps.
From Rzeszow they took us-- the women went to Auschwitz.
And the men-- some went to Gross-Rosen and to-- Mat-- Mauthausen?
Mauthausen, yes.
And in Gross-Rosen-- now, wait a minute.
They took us before these women.
Before we went to Auschwitz, we went to Krakau. 17 women went to Krakau to Plaszow, to this camp.
And we were there till this was already when the Germans were pushed by the Russians back west, you see?
And the war was nearly ending.
It was terrible.
Plaszow was to me a terrible camp, because you came there during the winter.
Nobody was dressed properly.
They didn't give us anything to eat.
In Plaszow, the people that were from Krakau, they knew all the ropes there.
They were from Krakau.
But we just came to Krakau to this camp.
We didn't know anybody.
I was working to clean the snow in the camp.
It was a terrible camp.
The German authorities there-- they had their henchmen.
Let's put it this way.
They were Jews that were on the top of this camp.
And they were organizing everything for the Germans, of course.
And you couldn't survive very long can in this camp if you were not from Krakau.
So finally, we were a little bit lucky later that they sent another German that his name was Schupka.
And he was before in Rzeszow.
So he knew my husband and me and few of those Jews.
So he helped us because then Krakau was just like us, you know?
But it was very bad.
This camp was terrible.
Describe what a day was like there.
A day?
A day and a night.
5 o'clock in the morning, we'd get up.
We were not dressed properly.
We went out.
And we cleaned the snow.
Some people were in there.
But later, when Schupka came, you see, they put us to work in the-- they have some work there for the Germans like [NON-ENGLISH].
So the people were sewing some.
Another one was like [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
So I finished that.
And that started to be a normal thing, you know?
They gave us once daily something to eat, which was dirty water with one potato in it.
And this is how we were doing.
And every day it was-- they called us to the platz, appellplatz.
And they were taking 5 people, 10 people, 100 people to send out.
You know, we didn't know too much about anything, because we were closed.
We were in the camps.
If you wanted to know something about war, and the camps, and so on, that took historians, because they were outside.
They were following the things.
But us, we were working in the camps.
That was all.
So once, finally, they started to liquidate this camp, Plaszow.
We knew that this a-- how do call it?
A factory, Emalia, that some people were going from Plaszow to Emalia and back.
And we knew there existed a German that is good trying to help those people.
But I never saw him before.

So finally, when they started to finish the camp to send out all the people, I forgot to tell you that in the meantime, they brought my husband to the same camp, Plaszow, again, where we were together.
So when they started to send out the people, they started to talk about some lists that the people are going to work to this Emalia.
And we were all standing there.
And they were calling the people from the list.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And my husband was standing next to this.
And he called my name.
I was not on the list.
But he called out.
I went to the right.
And they went to the transport.
We finished in these wagons.
And we came to Auschwitz.
This was Birkenau, because Auschwitz was divided into several camps.
And this was Birkenau.
Oh, it was closed-- no air with so many people in one wagon.
I was thinking that nobody will survive in this train.
It was like cattle-- very, very bad.
And then we came to Auschwitz.
Nobody, nobody tried to figure out how long.
You just were trying to survive two more minutes, 10 more minutes.
I couldn't tell you.
But it was a long ride from Plaszow to Auschwitz.
When we came to Auschwitz, it was terrible.
They took us.
They cut our hair.
They took all our clothes.
Nothing.
Nothing.
We didn't know too much.
We were closed.
We were closed.
We were interested in our work.
We were interested to get this soup.
And this was it.
We knew about the killing.
We knew that nobody will survive.
But I really cannot remember that I thought normally.
Nobody thought normally.
The people from Krakau-- they were in the group.
They knew many things.
Before we left Plaszow going to Auschwitz, the Germans killed all these collaborators.
And we were supposed to go there and look at them.
Practically everybody was killed.
Now, we had some people from the Jewish community in this camp-- they were trying to make a lot of money to get some people on the list, off the list.
But really, we didn't realize what this list meant.
No.
I heard the first time of Schindler when we-- when they brought the people from this factory back to our camp.
And we helped to undress them and to give them another type of clothes.
And I met my friend from Przemysl from school that I went with her all through school.
And she came from this factory.
She was working in there.
She married to Krakau, you see, before the war.
And I met her there.
And she taught me about Schindler.
And there was a few other Jews from Przemysl that they came.
And they told us that he is saving the Jews.
But I never met him.
Then when I came to Auschwitz, I came with this friend of mine.
All the time, we were together.
Her name was Rella Ast Zuckerman.
Her husband is still alive.
She died last year.
I didn't.
I tried not to.
No.
I knew about them.
I knew that they are--
No, no, no.
But everybody's trying like they could.
But this was among the people from Krakau, you know, the hierarchy.
They didn't pass my judgment about that.
We tried not to have too much to do.
And finally, I finished with the people from Krakau in the camp in Czechoslovakia, Schindler's camp.
But this was run by the Jews from Krakau.
Once.
Once I was swollen.
I went to the infirmary.
And I was a little bit to the side.
So the physician came out and give me one.
And I said, I never got anything from Germans.
But I got it from a Jew.
Yeah.
But I will tell you something.
The situation I could talk to you about for hours.
I was in Plaszow.
And I went to the infirmary to get something because I had a terrible cold.
So the doctor there-- you have told me.
You have to come here.
You are here.
I have to put you in the hospital.
OK, I won't have to go to work.
I went to this hospital.
My husband was not there yet.
In the evening, I hear a whistle.
We had our whistle, me and my husband.
I ran to the window.
And he told me I have TB, too.
So I ran to the window.
And my husband was there.
They brought the men from the other camp to this camp.
And he said, what are you doing there?
I said, I have TB.
He said, jump from the window, because everybody was closed.
Jump through the window.
I said, but I have TB.
He said, you don't have TB.
Jump!
And I jumped.
And the next morning, the Germans came and took everybody from this hospital and killed them.
Now, what this guy did-- and this guy is still alive-- this doctor-- they had a certain number of patients at the hospital.
So the Germans asked them we have to have these people from the hospital.
And they would take them, or send them out, or kill them.
And he said the next they, we   will come for them.
Now, they knew.
So when they grabbed a few patients and put into the hospital to save somebody else there-- that paid, or family, or what.
And they told me.
If not for my husband, I wouldn't be here today.
And he knew it.
What are you doing in the hospital?
By the way, I never had TB.
It was something like this that makes your life bearable anyway.
Yeah.
We were marching to the train because they were taking us to the train.
And the girl that I was working with-- I don't know if you think this.
She told me let's go backwards, not forwards.
So I started to go back, you know, the German-- the woman that was supervising this and hit me in the head, because I was going backwards.
So I have still a broken skull.
But it was not so bad, you know?
But I still was going backwards with this person.
She lives now.
And her name is Chanya Foyer.
And she lives in New York.
And we both went back first.
When we came next to the train, there was no place anymore.
So we stayed in the camp.
And they took the transport away.
Where did that one go to?
That one-- probably Auschwitz or something.
This was Auschwitz-Birkenau.
We stayed there something like a week.
And we gave up already.
And all of a sudden, Schindler came.
And he argued out his Jews that he wants to have them.
And in Auschwitz, they wanted to give him other Jews.
No, he wants his Jews.
And this was like he saved our life, you know?
They put into train.
And from Auschwitz, we went to-- what was the name?
What?
Brunnlitz?
Brunnlitz, yeah.
When he came there, our husbands were there already.
But they didn't recognize us.
I was standing next to my husband.
And he was calling me.
Yeah.
OK.
